Name: rate_distortion_model_compression
Title: Rate-Distortion Optimization for Adaptive Model Compression in Deep Learning
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formulating model compression as a rate-distortion optimization problem, we can derive adaptive compression schemes that dynamically allocate bits to model parameters based on their task-relevant information, leading to improved compression ratios and minimal performance degradation.
Related Work: Previous work has explored the connection between model compression and rate-distortion theory, primarily focusing on pruning and quantization techniques. Notably, Isik et al. (2021) and Bu et al. (2019) have shown that model pruning and compression can be viewed through the lens of rate-distortion theory, providing theoretical bounds on the trade-offs between compression ratio and model performance. However, these works often assume static compression strategies and do not adaptively allocate bits based on the task-relevant information of individual parameters. Our proposal aims to extend these ideas by developing an adaptive compression scheme that dynamically adjusts the bit allocation for each parameter, thereby achieving better compression-performance trade-offs.
Abstract: We propose a novel approach to model compression in deep learning by formulating it as a rate-distortion optimization problem. Unlike existing methods that often use static compression strategies, our approach dynamically allocates bits to model parameters based on their task-relevant information. This is achieved by deriving an adaptive compression scheme that minimizes the distortion in model performance while maintaining a specified bit budget. We leverage mutual information and Fisher information to estimate the task-relevance of each parameter and use these estimates to guide the compression process. Our method is supported by theoretical bounds on the achievable compression-performance trade-offs and is validated through experiments on standard benchmarks. The proposed approach not only improves compression ratios but also maintains or even enhances model performance, offering a principled and adaptive solution to model compression.

Experiments.Name: AdaptiveBitAllocation
Experiments.Description: We will implement an adaptive bit allocation scheme where the number of bits assigned to each parameter is determined by its task-relevant information, estimated using mutual information and Fisher information.
Experiments.Metrics: Compression ratio, model performance (accuracy, F1-score), and distortion (difference in performance before and after compression).
Experiments.Method: We will use a gradient-based approach to estimate the mutual information and Fisher information for each parameter. The bit allocation will be optimized to minimize the distortion while maintaining a specified bit budget. The optimization will be performed using a Lagrangian relaxation of the rate-distortion problem.

Experiments.Name: ComparisonWithStaticSchemes
Experiments.Description: We will compare the performance of our adaptive bit allocation scheme with static compression schemes such as uniform quantization and pruning.
Experiments.Metrics: Compression ratio, model performance, and distortion.
Experiments.Method: We will train deep neural networks on standard datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet) and apply both the adaptive and static compression schemes. The performance and distortion will be measured and compared.

Experiments.Name: TheoreticalBounds
Experiments.Description: We will derive theoretical bounds on the achievable compression-performance trade-offs for our adaptive compression scheme.
Experiments.Metrics: Achievable rate-distortion curves, theoretical limits on compression.
Experiments.Method: We will use information-theoretic tools such as rate-distortion theory and mutual information to derive the bounds. The bounds will be compared against empirical results from the experiments.

Risk Factors and Limitations: Estimating task-relevant information for each parameter can be computationally expensive and may introduce additional overhead.
The adaptive bit allocation scheme may not always outperform static schemes, especially in scenarios where the task-relevant information is uniformly distributed.
The theoretical bounds derived may be loose in practice, and further refinements may be necessary to tighten them.
The performance of the adaptive scheme may vary across different architectures and datasets, requiring additional experiments to validate its robustness.
